meta.title: D240 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Jaguar E-Pace D240 2.0 twin-turbo diesel — real UK costs, timing chain & DPF insight, and honest reliability data. Get free quotes from 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists today.

@graph.name: Jaguar E-Pace D240 Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Jaguar E-Pace D240 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned D240 twin-turbo diesel engine typically costs £4,000–£6,500 supply only. Used engines are available from £2,500–£4,500. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop.

mainEntity.name: Is the Jaguar E-Pace D240 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The D240 has a mixed reputation. The 2.0-litre Ingenium diesel is a powerful and efficient engine, but it's prone to timing chain stretch (particularly pre-2019 models), DPF clogging (urban use), and EGR cooler failures. In the What Car? reliability survey, the E-Pace came 29th out of 34 cars in the family SUV category. With proper maintenance (10,000-mile oil changes), the D240 can be a durable engine.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Jaguar E-Pace D240?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are timing chain stretch and tensioner wear (metallic rattle on cold start), DPF clogging (urban driving), oil dilution from failed regenerations, EGR cooler and valve failures, and AdBlue system issues. The E-Pace's firewall positioning can exacerbate DPF regeneration problems.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Jaguar E-Pace D240 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value. Against a well-kept £22,000 E-Pace D240, a £5,000 recon is 23% — worth it. Against a tired £9,000 example, the same recon approaches our 60% Jaguar threshold — get a scrap valuation first.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Jaguar E-Pace D240 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The D240 carries the engine code 204DTA (also referred to as AJ20D4 in some documentation). It's a 2.0-litre twin-turbo diesel engine used in the E-Pace X540 from 2017 to 2024. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'D240'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the timing chain, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative timing chain and tensioner replacement costs £800–£2,000. If ignored and the chain snaps, the engine is an interference design — pistons and valves collide, resulting in catastrophic engine damage. Repair bills can easily exceed £4,000–£8,000 depending on damage. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes (10,000-mile intervals) are the single most effective preventative measure.

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar E-Pace D240 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the 2.0-litre Ingenium twin-turbo diesel engine. Most Jaguar engine replacements range from £3,000 to £9,000 depending on the model and engine type. Used 204DTA engines typically sit in the £2,500–£4,500 band.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E-Pace D240 engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). The transverse engine layout and tight engine bay make this a moderately labour-intensive job.

cta.label: → Compare D240 engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch & Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Metallic rattle on cold start lasting a few seconds, engine management light, rough idle, loss of power, cam/crank correlation fault codes.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,000 (chain + tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £4,000–£7,500 (if chain snaps and pistons strike valves)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Timing chain stretch is the single most notorious issue on the Ingenium diesel. The chain system is far more sensitive to wear than older "lifetime" designs. If you hear a rattle even once — it's worth getting checked. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, get the chain and tensioners inspected immediately. If ignored, timing chain failure can lead to catastrophic engine damage and repair bills exceeding £4,000–£8,000. Regular oil changes (10,000-mile intervals) are the single most effective preventative measure — the factory 21,000-mile interval is too long for these engines.

failureLink.label: Read the full timing chain failure guide

problems.title: DPF Clogging & Oil Dilution
problems.symptoms: DPF warning light, reduced performance, increased fuel consumption, failed regeneration cycles, oil level rising on the dipstick (fuel dilution).
problems.repairCost: £300–£900 (forced regeneration, DPF clean, or oil change)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (replacement DPFs are pricey)
problems.recommendation: A blocked diesel particulate filter (DPF) is common on the Ingenium diesel. The E-Pace is particularly susceptible because the positioning of the firewall can exacerbate failed regeneration problems. Oil dilution can happen as a result of failed regenerations. If you predominantly drive in town, ensure the vehicle completes regular regeneration cycles — a motorway run at 2,000+ rpm for 20 minutes at least once a week is recommended.

failureLink.label: Read the full DPF failure guide

problems.title: EGR Cooler & Valve Failures
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white smoke from exhaust, engine management light, loss of power, limp mode.
problems.repairCost: £400–£800 (EGR cooler replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.recommendation: EGR valve and cooler failures are common on the Ingenium diesel. A blocked EGR valve can quickly lead to DPF issues and limp mode. If you're experiencing coolant loss or engine management warnings, have the EGR system inspected promptly. Ignoring it can lead to overheating and potential engine damage.

failureLink.label: Read the full EGR cooler failure guide

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: E-Pace D240 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Forced regeneration or DPF clean — less than replacement
Specialist repair available; cheaper than engine replacement
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This D240? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented service history with 10,000-mile (or less) oil changes using JLR-spec oil — the factory 21,000-mile interval is too long
* Evidence of DPF-friendly driving — regular motorway runs
* No cold-start rattle — a quiet startup suggests healthy timing chain
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle with no timing chain history
* No proof of regular oil changes — the Ingenium diesel is unforgiving of neglect
* DPF warning light with no regeneration history
* Engine warning light with timing, EGR, or DPF codes and no diagnosis

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E-Pace D240 offers the most powerful diesel engine in the range — 240 PS and 500 Nm from the twin-turbo Ingenium. It's a capable and responsive engine when running well. However, the Ingenium diesel family has a well-documented reputation for timing chain stretch, DPF clogging, and EGR issues. The E-Pace's transverse engine layout and firewall positioning can exacerbate DPF regeneration problems. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained D240 is a £18,000–£30,000 premium compact SUV; a neglected one is a £5,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare D240 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Jaguar E-Pace D240 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling or metallic ticking (timing chain stretch warning)
* Service History — including the D240 twin-turbo era (2017–2024) and confirmed oil-change intervals — ideally every 10,000 miles, not the 21,000-mile recommended interval
* DPF Check — Check for DPF warning light or evidence of regeneration issues (urban-driven vehicles are high risk)
* Oil Level — Check the dipstick; rising oil level indicates fuel dilution from failed DPF regenerations
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate EGR coolant leak
* Coolant Level — Any history of coolant loss without visible leaks? (EGR cooler warning)
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, EGR, DPF, or AdBlue issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
